title.emphasis: Part II: Deconstructing the Components: Legal and Financial Frameworks

section.para: To engineer a viable political cooperative security, one must navigate and synthesize four distinct and complex regulatory and structural domains: cooperative law, federal tax law, the mechanics of financial securitization, and the intricate web of campaign finance and political law. Each domain presents both significant constraints and potential opportunities. This part deconstructs these components to build a foundational understanding for the synthetic models proposed later in this report.

title.emphasis: Section 3: The Cooperative Legal & Tax Structure

section.para: The cooperative is a unique business entity, defined by a set of principles and a specific tax treatment that distinguish it from standard investor-owned firms. Understanding these foundational elements is critical, as they form the primary legal and financial chassis upon which any new model must be built.

The modern cooperative movement is guided by seven core principles, as articulated by the International Cooperative Alliance, which trace their roots to the Rochdale Pioneers of 1844.15 These principles are:

listitem.para: Voluntary and Open Membership: Cooperatives are voluntary organizations, open to all able to use their services and willing to accept the responsibilities of membership, without discrimination.17

Democratic Member Control: Cooperatives are democratic organizations controlled by their members, who actively participate in setting policies and making decisions. In primary cooperatives, this is embodied by the "one member, one vote" rule.15

Member Economic Participation: Members contribute equitably to, and democratically control, the capital of the cooperative. Benefits are distributed in proportion to members' transactions (patronage) with the cooperative, not on the capital invested.15
Autonomy and Independence: Cooperatives are autonomous, self-help organizations. If they raise external capital, they must do so on terms that ensure continued democratic control by members.15
Education, Training, and Information: Cooperatives provide education and training to enable effective member participation.15
Cooperation Among Cooperatives: Cooperatives strengthen the movement by working together.15
Concern for Community: Cooperatives work for the sustainable development of their communities.18

section.para: Of these, the principles of democratic member control and member economic participation are most salient to this analysis, as they directly conflict with the logic of a tradable, investor-oriented security.

title.emphasis: The Principle of Subordination of Capital

section.para: The most significant ideological and structural constraint within cooperative law is the principle of subordination of capital. This principle dictates that in a cooperative, "capital is the servant, not the master".3 Unlike a traditional corporation where the primary goal is to maximize return on investment for shareholders, a cooperative's purpose is to meet the needs of its members.3 Capital is merely a tool to achieve this end.

This principle manifests in several concrete ways. First, returns on equity investments are strictly limited. The IRS has affirmed that "a cooperative may not be operated for the purpose of paying a return on equity investments".3 Many state statutes and the Capper-Volstead Act explicitly limit dividends on equity to a low percentage, such as 8%.17 Second, control of the cooperative must remain with the member-patrons, not with non-patron equity investors.20 This is the essence of democratic control. Third, the pecuniary benefits of the cooperative's operations must primarily inure to the members based on their use (patronage), not to investors based on their capital contribution.20 This ensures the cooperative remains faithful to its purpose of providing services at cost, rather than generating profits for capital providers.20 This fundamental principle appears to create an insurmountable barrier to the creation of a security designed for capital appreciation and trade.

title.emphasis: Subchapter T of the Internal Revenue Code

section.para: The tax treatment of cooperatives is governed by Subchapter T of the Internal Revenue Code (§§ 1381-1388).22 This regime is built around the "single-tax principle," which recognizes the unique relationship between a cooperative and its patrons. Instead of the double taxation faced by C corporations (where profits are taxed at the corporate level and again when distributed as dividends to shareholders), Subchapter T allows a cooperative to be treated as a conduit, passing its earnings through to its patrons to be taxed only once, at the patron level.24

The mechanism for this pass-through is the patronage dividend (also called a patronage refund). A cooperative can deduct patronage dividends from its gross income, effectively eliminating its own tax liability on that income.22 However, this favorable treatment is strictly limited to a specific type of income. This creates the central, and most critical, distinction in cooperative tax law: the separation of patronage-sourced income from non-patronage-sourced income.

The ability to deduct patronage dividends applies only to income derived from patronage sources. This distinction is the linchpin of the entire Subchapter T framework and, as will be shown, the key to unlocking the political cooperative model.

listitem.para: Patronage-Sourced Income: The Code defines this as earnings derived from "business done with or for its patrons".24 To be deductible, distributions from this income must be paid to patrons based on the quantity or value of their business with the co-op, pursuant to a pre-existing legal obligation.24 This is the income stream subject to the principle of subordination of capital.

Non-Patronage-Sourced Income: This is any other income the cooperative earns. Treasury Regulation § 1.1382-3(c)(2) defines it as "incidental income derived from sources not directly related to marketing, purchasing, or service activities of the cooperative association".24 Examples given include income from the lease of premises, investments in securities, and the sale of capital assets.24 This income is not eligible for the patronage dividend deduction. It is taxed at the cooperative level at regular corporate rates, just like any C corporation, and is not subject to the cooperative principles of distribution based on patronage or subordination of capital.23

section.para: For years, the IRS and cooperatives battled over how to classify income that wasn't explicitly listed in the regulations. The IRS often took a literal, per se view, arguing that any income from investments, for example, was automatically non-patronage. Cooperatives argued for a more functional approach.24 Over time, court decisions have solidified the cooperative position, establishing the "directly related/actually facilitates" test as the proper standard.24

This test is a fact-intensive inquiry into the economic reality of a transaction.24 It asks whether the income-generating activity is "integrally intertwined" with and "actually facilitates" the accomplishment of the cooperative's primary business of serving its patrons.24 For example, interest earned by a cooperative on a loan from a Bank for Cooperatives, where the loan was taken out to finance the acquisition of supplies for its members, was deemed patronage-sourced because borrowing the money "actually facilitates" the cooperative's purchasing activities.24 Conversely, if a transaction "merely enhances the overall profitability" but has no necessary linkage to the cooperative enterprise, the income is from non-patronage sources.24

title.emphasis: Subchapter T as an Enabling Framework

section.para: At first glance, the rigid principles of cooperative law and the complex rules of Subchapter T appear to be formidable obstacles. The subordination of capital seems to forbid the creation of a profit-seeking security, and the democratic control principle appears to preclude the influence of outside investors. However, a deeper analysis reveals that the structure of Subchapter T, particularly its strict bifurcation of income, does not prohibit the proposed model but in fact provides a precise legal and tax blueprint for its creation.

The key is to recognize that the constraints of cooperative principles apply only to patronage-sourced income. The existence of a separate, legally distinct category of non-patronage income, which is treated for tax and distribution purposes like the income of any standard corporation, creates a firewalled space within the cooperative structure.
The logical progression is as follows:

listitem.para: The goal is to create a tradable, appreciative security linked to a cooperative, which requires generating profits for capital investors.

Cooperative principles like subordination of capital and distribution based on patronage make it impossible to distribute profits from the cooperative's primary, member-serving business to investors based on their capital contribution.3

Subchapter T explicitly defines and segregates non-patronage income, which is not subject to these principles. This income is taxed at the corporate level, and any after-tax profits can be distributed as dividends to shareholders or retained, at the discretion of the board, without regard to patronage.23

Therefore, if a political cooperative can structure its political influence activities in such a way that the resulting revenue is legally classified as non-patronage income, it creates a pool of profits that is legally "free" from the constraints of traditional cooperative ideology. This would require demonstrating that the political activity does not "actually facilitate" the primary patronage business of the members but is instead a separate, incidental, profit-making endeavor.

This pool of non-patronage profits can then be allocated to a separate class of capital investors without violating Subchapter T or the foundational principles of cooperation as they apply to the patronage side of the business.

section.para: This realization transforms Subchapter T from a restrictive tax code into an architectural guide. It allows for the design of a hybrid entity that operates on a cooperative basis for its members while simultaneously operating a non-patronage business line for the benefit of its investors.

title.emphasis: Section 4: The Architecture of Liquidity: Securitization and Secondary Markets

section.para: To create a tradable cooperative security, it is not enough to simply generate a profit stream for investors. That profit stream must be packaged into a financial instrument that can be bought and sold with ease, providing the liquidity that investors demand. The field of structured finance, specifically the process of securitization, offers a powerful and well-established toolkit for transforming illiquid assets into marketable securities. This section outlines the mechanics of securitization and secondary markets, demonstrating how they can be adapted to serve the unique goals of the political cooperative.

Securitization is a financial process that pools various types of contractual debt or other income-producing assets and sells their related cash flows to third-party investors as securities. This process effectively transforms illiquid assets, such as mortgages or auto loans, into liquid, tradable instruments. While traditionally applied to debt, the principles can be applied to any asset with a stable and predictable future cash flow. The process typically involves several key steps:

listitem.para: Origination and Pooling: The process begins with an "originator"—in this case, the political cooperative—which identifies an income-producing asset it wishes to securitize. This asset would be the future net income stream generated from the cooperative's non-patronage political activities. The originator pools these assets or receivables into a "reference portfolio". For this model, the "pool" would be the contractual right to receive all future profits from the designated non-patronage business line.

The Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV): The originator then establishes a new, legally distinct entity known as a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) or Special Purpose Entity (SPE). This entity is created for the sole purpose of the securitization transaction. The originator sells the asset pool to the SPV in what is known as a "true sale". This is a critical legal step. A defining feature of the SPV is that it is structured to be "bankruptcy remote" from the originator. This means that if the originator (the cooperative) were to face financial distress or bankruptcy, the assets legally owned by the SPV are isolated and protected, available only to satisfy the claims of the investors who bought securities from the SPV. This legal insulation is a primary benefit, as it allows the securities to be rated based on the credit quality of the isolated assets, not the overall creditworthiness of the originator.

Issuance and Tranching: The SPV finances its purchase of the assets by issuing tradable, interest-bearing securities to investors in the capital markets. To appeal to a broad range of investors with different risk appetites, these securities are often structured into different layers, or "tranches".
Senior Tranches: These have the first claim on the cash flows from the underlying assets. They carry the lowest risk and offer the lowest yields, often receiving high credit ratings.
Mezzanine Tranches: These have a middle-level claim, absorbing losses only after the junior tranches are wiped out. They offer moderate risk and moderate yields.
Junior/Equity Tranches: These have the last claim on cash flows and are the first to absorb losses. They carry the highest risk and offer the highest potential returns, and are often unrated.

Ongoing Servicing: A "servicer," which is often the original lender or originator, continues to manage the underlying assets, collect payments, and pass them on (less a fee) to the SPV or a trustee, who then distributes the cash flows to the investors according to the tranche priority.

section.para: The value of a security is intrinsically linked to its liquidity—the ability of an investor to sell it for cash. While the initial issuance of securities occurs in the primary market, the vast majority of trading happens in secondary markets.27 These markets, which include centralized exchanges like the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and over-the-counter (OTC) markets, allow investors to buy and sell previously issued securities from one another, rather than from the issuing company.

For a novel instrument like a political cooperative security, establishing a liquid secondary market would be a significant challenge. Private secondary markets exist for the shares of non-public companies, but they are often opaque, illiquid, and subject to transfer restrictions. Platforms like Coop Exchange are emerging with the goal of creating dedicated secondary markets for cooperative shares, aiming to provide the liquidity, transparency, and flexibility that have historically been absent in the sector.29 The success of a tradable political cooperative security would depend heavily on the development of such a marketplace.

title.emphasis: The SPV as a Legal and Political Firewall

section.para: The architecture of securitization offers a solution that goes far beyond mere financial engineering; it provides a mechanism to resolve the central governance conflict of the political cooperative. The "bankruptcy remote" status of the SPV can be repurposed to create a robust political and legal firewall between the cooperative and its external investors.
This structure functions as follows:

listitem.para: The core problem is how to provide investors with a tradable, profit-generating instrument without giving them ownership or control rights that would violate the cooperative principle of democratic member control.
The securitization process, through the "true sale" of an asset to an SPV, legally severs the originator's connection to that asset.

In this model, the political cooperative (the Originator) would sell its future non-patronage income stream (the asset) to a newly created SPV. At this point, the legal ownership of that future income transfers entirely to the SPV.

The SPV, a separate legal person, then issues securities to investors. The investors' contract and legal relationship are exclusively with the SPV. Their claim is on the assets held by the SPV—the right to the future non-patronage income—not on the cooperative itself.

This structure elegantly achieves the user's primary objectives. The cooperative receives a significant infusion of upfront capital from the sale of the asset to the SPV. The investors receive a tradable security whose value is tied to the performance of the cooperative's political activities. Crucially, the cooperative's governance remains untouched. The investors are not members of the cooperative; they hold no cooperative shares and have no voting rights in its affairs. Their legal recourse is limited to the SPV and its assets.

section.para: This use of the SPV as a firewall effectively insulates the cooperative's democratic core from the demands of external capital. It allows the cooperative to access public capital markets while preserving its fundamental identity, resolving the "capital conundrum" in a novel and powerful way.

The creation of a political cooperative cannot occur in a vacuum. It must be designed to operate within the specific legal and regulatory framework governing political activity by corporate entities in the United States. This framework has been fundamentally reshaped over the past two decades by landmark Supreme Court decisions that have expanded the concept of corporate rights and by the subsequent proliferation of new vehicles for political spending.
The legal landscape of corporate political speech rests on two intertwined doctrines: corporate personhood and the application of First Amendment rights to these "persons."

listitem.para: Legal Foundation of Corporate Personhood: The concept that a corporation is a "juridical person" with legal rights and responsibilities separate from its owners or managers is a long-standing principle in law.31 U.S. law has long recognized corporations as legal entities that can hold property, enter into contracts, and sue or be sued.32 While the Constitution does not explicitly mention corporations, the Supreme Court has, since the 19th century, recognized that they are holders of certain constitutional rights.32 This doctrine does not grant corporations all the rights of natural persons, but it establishes them as entities capable of asserting constitutional protections.32

Citizens United v. FEC
(2010): This seminal Supreme Court case dramatically altered the landscape of campaign finance. In a 5-4 decision, the Court held that the government cannot restrict independent political spending by corporations and unions, as doing so violates the First Amendment's protection of free speech.34 The majority opinion, authored by Justice Kennedy, asserted that the government may not suppress political speech based on the speaker's corporate identity.35 The Court reasoned that "independent" spending—that which is not coordinated with a candidate's campaign—does not give rise to the quid pro quo corruption that would justify such a restriction.36 The decision overruled prior precedents that had allowed for such bans.38 The dissent, authored by Justice Stevens, argued passionately that the ruling "threatens to undermine the integrity of elected institutions" by allowing corporate domination of the electoral process, rejecting the notion that corporations are members of society in the same way as individuals.36

Burwell v. Hobby Lobby
(2014): This decision further solidified the trend of ascribing rights to corporate entities. The Court ruled 5-4 that "closely held" for-profit corporations could be exempt from a federal mandate to provide contraceptive coverage to their employees if it violated the owners' sincere religious beliefs.40 The legal basis for this was the Religious Freedom Restoration Act (RFRA), which prohibits the government from substantially burdening a "person's" exercise of religion. The Court, citing the U.S. Code's "dictionary" definition, found that "person" includes corporations, thus extending RFRA's protections to these entities.42 This case reinforced the legal principle that corporations can be vehicles for the expression of their owners' rights and beliefs.

section.para: Together, these cases establish a legal environment where a corporate entity, including an incorporated cooperative, possesses a robust First Amendment right to engage in unlimited independent political spending.
In the wake of Citizens United, a sophisticated ecosystem of political financing vehicles has emerged, each with distinct rules and capabilities.

listitem.para: Political Action Committees (PACs): Often called "connected PACs" or "separate segregated funds" (SSFs), these are established by corporations, labor unions, or trade groups.43 They are funded by voluntary contributions from a restricted class (e.g., managers and shareholders of a corporation) and are subject to strict contribution limits (e.g., $5,000 per candidate per election).43 They can, however, contribute directly to a candidate's campaign committee.44

Super PACs (Independent Expenditure-Only Committees): Created as a direct result of the Citizens United and SpeechNow.org v. FEC decisions, Super PACs can raise unlimited sums of money from individuals, corporations, and unions.37 They can then spend unlimited amounts to expressly advocate for or against candidates. The crucial legal restriction is that their spending must be truly "independent" and cannot be coordinated with candidates or political parties.45

501(c)(4) "Social Welfare" Organizations: These are tax-exempt organizations that must be "operated exclusively for the promotion of social welfare".47 Under IRS rules, this means they must be "primarily engaged" in promoting the common good and general welfare of the community.48 The IRS has established a "primary activity test" to make this determination, but it is a vague "facts and circumstances" test with no clear numerical threshold, though a cautious guideline is that political campaign intervention should not exceed 30-40% of total expenditures.49 Unlike 501(c)(3) charities, 501(c)(4)s can engage in unlimited lobbying related to their mission and a certain amount of political campaign activity.51

section.para: The most significant consequence of this regulatory structure is the rise of "dark money." This term refers to political spending by groups that are not required to disclose their donors.53 The primary vehicle for this is the 501(c)(4) organization.

The mechanism works as follows: A wealthy individual or corporation makes a large, anonymous donation to a 501(c)(4) organization. Because the 501(c)(4) is generally not required to publicly disclose its donors, the source of the funds remains secret.47 That 501(c)(4) can then make a large contribution to a Super PAC. The Super PAC must disclose that it received money from the 501(c)(4), but the trail ends there; the original donor remains hidden from public view.53 This creates a perfectly legal channel for anonymous, unlimited spending to influence elections, a practice that has been used extensively by groups on both sides of the political spectrum.55

title.emphasis: Legal Status of Cooperatives in Campaign Finance

section.para: Federal Election Commission (FEC) regulations explicitly address the role of cooperatives. An incorporated cooperative is treated like any other corporation and is therefore prohibited from using its general treasury funds to make direct contributions to federal candidates.57 However, like other corporations, a cooperative is legally permitted to use its treasury funds to establish, administer, and solicit contributions for a separate segregated fund (SSF)—a traditional PAC.57 This PAC can then make limited contributions to candidates.

The Citizens United decision overlays this existing framework. It grants cooperatives, as corporate entities, the First Amendment right to make unlimited independent expenditures directly from their treasury funds. Therefore, a political cooperative could legally fund advertisements, get-out-the-vote efforts, or other communications expressly advocating for or against a candidate, provided such spending is not coordinated with any campaign.58 This gives the political cooperative a direct and powerful avenue for political engagement, funded by its operational revenues.
